title: The Caregiver Is 75. The Person Receiving Care Is 75.

paragraphs: We often picture caregiving as something done by healthy adult children. But that picture now shows only part of reality.
An older husband supports his older wife. An older wife supports her older husband.
In 37.1 percent of cases, both the person receiving care and the main caregiver living with them are 75 or older. That is the highest level ever recorded and well over one in three.

The number fits in a single line. But inside that line are people helping a partner despite back pain, waking up night after night, and going to the hospital while managing health problems of their own.
The person giving support may already need support too.

subsections.heading: The idea that caregivers are healthy is breaking down
paragraphs: Caregivers prepare meals, help someone get dressed, wake up during the night and take family members to the hospital.
Each task may look small, but when the tasks continue every day, they drain physical strength. The caregiver may also be getting weaker with age or living with health problems.
If we look only at the person receiving care, we may miss the risk facing the whole household.

subsections.heading: Family love should not carry the full weight of care
paragraphs: The wish to help because “we are married” or “we are family” is real. But love alone cannot remove exhaustion or back pain.
Home-care workers, day services, short stays and local support centers can share the work. Using outside help does not mean giving up your role in the family.
It is another form of care that can stop both people from reaching their limit together.

sections.heading: In One Line
paragraphs: The shoulders carrying the burden of care already need support themselves.

The figure of 37.1 percent shows more than the strength of family bonds. When care stays entirely inside the home, both the caregiver and the person receiving care can move toward their limit at the same time.
